title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Location
PDS 70 is located at a distance of 113.43 ± 0.52 pc and is a member of the Scorpius–Centaurus stellar association, specifically of the Upper Centaurus–Lupus (UCL) subgroup. One of the pieces of evidence that this is a young system is that, as the authors note, “Strong lithium absorption and the presence of a protoplanetary disk provide evidence of a young age” (<10 Myr) for PDS 70 (Gregorio-Hetem and Hetem 2002; Metchev and Hillenbrand 2004).

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
General Description of the System
PDS 70 is a young system composed of a pre-main-sequence T Tauri star of spectral type K7, surrounded by a transition protoplanetary disk. It is an ideal laboratory for studying planet formation because it brings together, within a single system, an inner disk, a large gap, an outer ring, and protoplanets.
The inner disk is a compact component located very close to the star, inside the large gap. Earlier observations described it as “a small optically thick inner disk emitting in the NIR” (Hashimoto et al. 2012; Dong et al. 2012). Later, this component was shown to be detected in the continuum within ~9 au and in gas out to ~15 au (Keppler et al. 2019), indicating that the innermost region still retains material close to the star.
The large gap corresponds to the radial cavity that separates the inner disk from the outer disk. Its structure was confirmed when the authors stated that “we confirm the detection of a large gap of ∼54 au in size within the disk in our scattered light images” (Keppler et al. 2018). The gap also shows evidence of radial dust segregation, since “The gap is found to be larger at sub-mm wavelengths” (Keppler et al. 2019).
The outer disk, in turn, is observed as a bright ring whose main emission peak is located at “The best-fit model has a peak radial position of 73.7 ± 0.1 au” (Keppler et al. 2019).
Finally, the system is especially remarkable because of the direct detection of protoplanets within the gap. Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2018) reported “a point source within the gap of the disk at about 195 mas (∼22 au) projected separation”, which was identified as PDS 70 b. Later, Haffert et al. (Haffert et al. 2019) detected Hα emission at two locations in the system and concluded: “We therefore identify this second source of Hα emission as the location of a second, forming planet, PDS 70 c”. The evidence for active accretion in both objects is based on the fact that “The Hα emission spectra of both proto-planets indicate ongoing accretion onto the proto-planets” (Haffert et al. 2019).
Overall, PDS 70 stands out as a valuable laboratory for planet formation because it combines a large cavity, an inner disk, a well-resolved outer ring, and two actively accreting protoplanets within the gap.

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Disk Morphology
PDS 70 exhibits the characteristic morphology of a transition disk, but with an especially rich internal structure. The system includes a compact inner disk, a large cavity, and a well-resolved outer ring. In polarized light, the outer disk appears as an elliptical ring with a non-uniform brightness distribution. ALMA observations, however, reveal a more complex arrangement of dust and gas, including a main ring at ~74 au, a possible additional inner component, and a tentative bridge- or spur-like structure that may connect the outer and inner regions of the disk. Located within the large gap are the two protoplanets PDS 70 b and PDS 70 c, which makes this system a particularly important case for studying planet–disk interaction and planet formation in progress.

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Dust in the disk
The dust in PDS 70 is not evenly distributed, but instead organized into distinct disk substructures. Beyond the inner disk lies the gap, a region where the dust emission is strongly depleted. Farther out, ALMA millimeter continuum observations, which trace larger dust grains, show that this material is primarily concentrated in an outer ring peaking at 73.7 ± 0.1 au, with a possible additional component near 60 au. Taken together, these observations indicate that small grains remain closer to the cavity edge, whereas larger grains accumulate farther out, consistent with a dust-trapping scenario (Keppler et al. 2018, 2019).

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Gas in the disk
The gas component in PDS 70 has been studied primarily through the \(^{12}\)CO \(J=3-2\) line. Unlike the dust continuum, this emission traces an elevated layer of the disk rather than the midplane, so its observed morphology appears more extended and geometrically more complex. ALMA observations show that the gaseous cavity is not completely empty: there is an inner depression in emission near 23 au, an inner gaseous disk extending out to about 15 au, and a possible structure linking the inner disk to the outer ring in the northwestern region. In addition, the gas kinematics show departures from Keplerian rotation inside ~0.8″ (91 au), suggesting the presence of pressure gradients within the disk. Taken together, these results indicate that the gas is depleted near the orbit of PDS 70 b, but not absent, and may still be flowing between the outer disk and the inner regions (Keppler et al. 2019).

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
PDS 70 provides some of the clearest evidence of ongoing planet formation in a protoplanetary disk. First, Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2018) detected PDS 70 b as a point source inside the large disk gap, at a separation of about 195 mas (~22 au), and follow-up observations confirmed that it is physically associated with the system. Its photometry is also consistent with a planetary-mass companion. Second, Haffert et al. (Haffert et al. 2019) detected Hα emission from both PDS 70 b and PDS 70 c. Because Hα traces accreting gas, this shows that both protoplanets are still actively gaining material and continue to grow. Third, Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2019) found that the disk itself is being perturbed: the CO emission shows a depression near the orbit of PDS 70 b, together with a deviation from Keplerian rotation that reveals changes in the gas pressure structure. Taken together, these results show that PDS 70 is not only a disk with substructures, but a system where planet formation is directly observed in action.

title: PDS 70:Protoplanetary disk
text:
Physical interpretation
PDS 70 is interpreted as a young planetary system that is still forming, in which the protoplanets are already actively reshaping the disk structure. The large cavity, the segregation between small and large dust grains, and the presence of an inner disk led Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2018) to identify the system early on as a strong candidate for harboring planets capable of carving the gap. Later, ALMA observations showed that the gas displays a depression near the orbit of PDS 70 b, together with a departure from Keplerian rotation consistent with a pressure gradient that can explain why the main dust ring lies much farther out; however, Keppler et al. (Keppler et al. 2019) also concluded that a single planet may not be enough to reproduce the full observed morphology, suggesting a more complex system architecture, possibly including an additional low-mass companion. The later Hα detection of PDS 70 c further strengthens this picture: both protoplanets are accreting, and their orbits are consistent with a near 2:1 resonance configuration, making PDS 70 a particularly valuable system for studying planet–disk interaction and migration in young planetary systems (Haffert et al. 2019).

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
IM Lup is located in “the Lupus 2 cloud” (Cleeves et al. 2016). Within this star-forming environment, the system lies at a distance of “d = 161 ± 10 pc” (Cleeves et al. 2016). The same study also reports “large-scale (>30″) CO emission from the parent cloud” (Cleeves et al. 2016), indicating that the disk is embedded in a broader natal environment.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
General description of the system
IM Lup is a young system located in the Lupus 2 cloud, at a distance of 161 ± 10 pc, whose central star has an estimated age of 0.5–1 Myr and a spectral type M0 (Cleeves et al. 2016). In general terms, this is a particularly interesting disk because earlier observations detected a gaseous disk together with “large-scale (>30″) CO emission from the parent cloud,” suggesting that the system still retains a connection to its natal environment (Cleeves et al. 2016). In addition, the disk was described as “massive, ∼0.1 M⊙,” a striking property because “IM Lup does not appear to be actively accreting,” although the same study notes that its accretion may vary over time (Cleeves et al. 2016). It is also notable that “the gas is more extended than the dust, 900 au versus 400 au,” which made IM Lup “one of the first systems where this dust–gas dichotomy was clearly seen” (Cleeves et al. 2016). This overall picture is reinforced by later studies showing that “The SPHERE and ALMA data show two very different geometries of the disk” (Franceschi et al. 2023), indicating that the system displays a tracer-dependent structure: more flared and vertically extended in the near-infrared, and flatter at millimeter wavelengths. Taken together, these properties show that IM Lup is not only a large and material-rich disk, but also a key system for studying how gas and dust can become distributed differently during the early stages of protoplanetary disk evolution.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
In morphological terms, IM Lup shows in the submillimeter continuum “a bright central continuum peak, and fainter emission which extends out as a broad plateau” (Cleeves et al. 2016), that is, a bright inner region followed by fainter and more extended outer emission. In addition, “The outer disk appears truncated at ∼2″” (Cleeves et al. 2016), suggesting that the observable material does not extend indefinitely outward. Superimposed on this global structure, the continuum profile exhibits “Minor breaks in the continuum slope suggestive of ring-like structures” (Cleeves et al. 2016), with discontinuities near 150 au and 250 au, indicating that the disk is not completely smooth but instead may host annular substructures. This picture becomes more concrete in scattered-light measurements, which report four small-grain rings at approximately 91.9, 152.1, 240.8, and 332.8 au (Rich et al. 2021), consistent with a sequence of concentric structures distributed across the disk. However, because “Higher-resolution observations are necessary to confirm the presence of these rings” (Cleeves et al. 2016), these features should for now be interpreted as tentative evidence for rings rather than as definitively confirmed ring structures.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
In IM Lup, dust is not distributed uniformly, but instead arranged in a complex structure with a bright inner region and a fainter, more extended outer component: the continuum shows “a bright central continuum peak, and fainter emission which extends out as a broad plateau” (Cleeves et al. 2016). Overall, the large grains are more concentrated than the gas, with a smaller vertical scale height, a more compact radial distribution, and a millimeter-grain disk truncated at 313 ± 15 au, so the system can be described as having two main components: a bright inner disk and a broad outer halo (Cleeves et al. 2016). In addition, the dust in the inner disk is dense enough to hide part of the line emission, suggesting the presence of large grains that are vertically suspended in this central region (Cleeves et al. 2016). This picture is reinforced by later work showing that within ~20 au, the surface density of large dust is “10–100 times higher than expected from an extrapolation of the outer disk” and that this region contains “a mass reservoir of at least 540 M⊕ within 20 au” (Bosman et al. 2023). To explain these observations, Bosman et al. (Bosman et al. 2023) propose that the large grains in the inner disk must be significantly vertically extended, to the point that “the dust scale height is the same as the gas scale height within 30 au” (Bosman et al. 2023). Taken together, these results show that IM Lup combines a globally settled millimeter-dust disk with an inner region that is optically thick, massive, and vertically more extended, making it a particularly interesting system for studying the early evolution of solids in protoplanetary disks. # # Dust properties

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
In IM Lup, the gas forms the most extended component of the disk. Observations show that 12CO is detected out to 970 au, while the millimeter dust is confined to a much more compact region, indicating that the gaseous disk extends far beyond the large-grain disk (Cleeves et al. 2016). At the observed scales, this gas appears to be distributed relatively smoothly, and the same study explains that in the outer regions, the lower dust content allows more radiation to penetrate, helping CO remain in the gas phase at large distances. From a chemical point of view, the authors estimate a gas-phase CO abundance equal to only 5% of the interstellar value, suggesting that CO is depleted, although it still remains an important tracer of the disk (Cleeves et al. 2016). They also find that IM Lup is exposed to a low external radiation field ((G_0 )), which nevertheless influences the temperature and chemistry of the gas, especially in the outer disk (Cleeves et al. 2016). In addition, they propose the presence of an extended diffuse halo of 12CO, suggesting that part of the gas may extend beyond the main disk, perhaps as a tenuous envelope or a gaseous continuation of the system’s surroundings (Cleeves et al. 2016). As a complement, Rich et al. (2021) show that the 12CO-emitting layer also has a flared vertical structure, with (= 1.77 ) and (H_0 = 22.7 ) au at 100 au, and that beyond 100 au its height exceeds that of the small-dust scattering surface (Rich et al. 2021). In other words, in the outer disk the gas not only extends farther out, but also remains at higher layers of the disk than the small dust grains.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
In IM Lup, the evidence for planet formation does not come from a directly confirmed planet, but rather from the way the disk itself appears to be evolving. Bosman et al. (Bosman et al. 2023) argue that “the IM Lup disk shows evidence that it has been shaped by an episode of dust drift,” meaning that the system was likely shaped by radial dust drift, a key process because it transports pebbles and solids through different regions of the disk. From this picture, the same study concludes that “This scenario is ideal for the formation of giant planet cores at larger (>20 au) radii” and that “The more settled regions outside 20 au are still conducive to giant planet formation” (Bosman et al. 2023), suggesting that the outer disk provides the most favorable conditions for the growth of giant-planet cores. This idea becomes especially important when the authors state that “The conditions in the IM Lup disk imply that giant planet formation through pebble accretion is only effective outside of 20 au” (Bosman et al. 2023), because that sentence directly links the observed disk structure to a physical consequence: in IM Lup, giant planet formation is not equally likely throughout the system, but appears to be favored mainly in the outer disk. Taken together, these results make IM Lup a particularly valuable case for understanding how dust evolution can point to specific regions where giant planet formation may be most efficient.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of IM Lup
In physical terms, IM Lup appears to be a disk whose evolution is governed by the radial and vertical redistribution of dust. Bosman et al. (Bosman et al. 2023) propose that the system was shaped by an intense episode of dust drift, which would have transported a large amount of solids inward and created a clear division between two disk regions: an inner zone, within ~20 au, where dust is strongly concentrated and remains vertically extended, and an outer zone where solid material is still drifting inward. This interpretation is important because it suggests that the inner disk does not behave like a quiet, settled region, but rather like a more dynamically active environment, where the high dust concentration and strong vertical mixing point to a turbulent state, possibly linked to the vertical shear instability. At the same time, Cleeves et al. (2016) show that the disk must be understood as a system in which gas, dust, and the external environment are tightly coupled: when dust is reduced in the outer disk, that region becomes more exposed to stellar and external radiation, the freeze-out rate changes, and the gas chemistry is altered; by contrast, the inner region is so dense and optically thick that it can hide part of the line emission, reinforcing the idea of a compact, opaque, and vertically complex inner disk. Taken together, this physical picture suggests that IM Lup is not a uniform disk, but a system in which different regions evolve in different ways: the inner disk would be dominated by dust pileup, high opacity, and turbulence, whereas the outer disk would preserve conditions more favorable for pebble accretion and, therefore, for the formation of giant-planet cores.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
HD 163296 is a young Herbig Ae star located at a distance of approximately 101–122 parsecs (~330–400 light-years) from Earth (Isella et al. 2016; Rab et al. 2020).
The star belongs to the class of intermediate-mass pre–main sequence stars and is surrounded by one of the most extensively studied protoplanetary disks. Because the disk is large, bright, and relatively isolated, it provides an ideal laboratory for investigating the processes that shape planet-forming disks.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
General description of the system
HD 163296 is a protoplanetary disk surrounding a Herbig Ae star whose dust morphology has made it a benchmark system for the study of radial substructures; indeed, it has been described as “the third system known to host multiple rings in dust emission at millimetre wavelength” (Diep et al. 2019). On global scales, the system combines a large, dynamically ordered gas disk—“The gas disc is in Keplerian motion and has a radius of about 550 au, and the millimetre continuum emission from solid particles is confined to within 250 au of the star”—with a more compact solid component, where earlier observations resolved “three concentric gap-ring pairs,” with gaps at ~54, ~100, and 160 au (Diep et al. 2019). Higher-resolution observations further show that this architecture is not limited to simple concentric rings: “The continuum observations resolve and allow us to characterize the previously discovered dust rings at radii of 68 and 100. They also reveal new small-scale structures, such as a dark gap at 10 au, a bright ring at 15 au, a dust crescent at a radius of 55 au, and several fainter azimuthal asymmetries” (Isella et al. 2018). In this sense, the dust distribution is not uniform but exhibits both radial and azimuthal variations, since “The dust continuum emission is characterized by several asymmetric structures,” and these features “corroborate the hypothesis that the complex structure of the HD 163296 disk is the result of the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets” (Isella et al. 2018).

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
The morphology of HD 163296 can be described as that of a dust disk with a hierarchical architecture of rings and gaps: in the 1.3 mm continuum, the disk shows “three concentric gap-ring pairs,” and at higher resolution it presents “much deeper gaps” between the rings, more clearly defining the radial redistribution of dust (Diep et al. 2019). To this global structure, Isella et al. (2018) add “three new morphological features,” including an inner dark gap at ~10 au, a bright ring at ~15 au, and a central azimuthal asymmetry, while in the outer disk “the outermost features B67, D86, B100, D145, and B155, are well described by concentric circles.” Beyond this concentric organization, the disk is not perfectly uniform: “the image of the residuals shows fainter asymmetries along the B67 and B100 dust rings” (Isella et al. 2018), and Diep et al. (2019) further conclude that the outer gap and outer ring display a “significant modulation of their morphology and intensity.” Altogether, HD 163296 exhibits a morphology in which gaps, rings, and local asymmetries coexist within a globally thin and well-organized disk structure.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
In the case of HD 163296, dust dynamics—that is, the radial migration of solids toward local gas-pressure maxima—is interpreted as a key mechanism in the formation of the rings. These bright rings—“the rings B67 and B100 of the HD 163296 disk, have a radial width that is narrower than the estimated pressure scale height of the gaseous disk” (Isella et al. 2018)—suggest that the dust is concentrated and confined within relatively narrow regions. In addition to ring and gap structures, the disk also shows asymmetric dust concentrations: “new small-scale structures, such as a dark gap at 10 au, a bright ring at 15 au, a dust crescent at a radius of 55 au, and several fainter azimuthal asymmetries” (Isella et al. 2018). Among these asymmetries, the most prominent correspond to crescents, since “The dust continuum emission is characterized by several asymmetric structures. The most prominent consist in crescents centered at about 4 and 55 au” (Isella et al. 2018). For this reason, the dusty environment of the disk should be understood as dynamic rather than perfectly axisymmetric. This interpretation is further reinforced by the kinematic evidence presented by Pinte et al. (2020), who note that “upon deprojection, we find that all of the candidate planets lie within a gap and/or at the end of a spiral detected in dust continuum emission.” In other words, the dust substructures not only describe where solid material is concentrated or redistributed, but also spatially coincide with possible embedded perturbers, supporting the idea that part of the observed dust architecture is being sculpted by forming protoplanets.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
According to the gas structure of HD 163296, the gaseous disk does not end where the millimeter dust continuum is observed, but instead extends much farther outward: “The CO emission extends far beyond the outer edge of millimeter-wave continuum emission up to an angular distance of 5 6, or about 560 au, from the central star” (Isella et al. 2018). To understand how its geometry is observed, it is important to note that the CO-traced gas does not appear as a single layer, but as two warm emitting surfaces on the front and rear sides of the disk: “The CO emission observed at intermediate velocities … shows a characteristic ‘dragonfly’ structure tracing both the front and rear warm CO-emitting layers of the disk” (Isella et al. 2018). The rear-side emission appears fainter because “The emission coming from the rear of the disk is fainter than that from the front due to a combination of lower temperature of the emitting gas and absorption from the intervening material” (Isella et al. 2018). The separation between these two surfaces therefore makes it possible to infer the vertical structure of the gas, since “the separation between the front and rear CO emission measures the vertical geometry of the CO-emitting surfaces” (Isella et al. 2018). In addition, the CO temperature decreases with distance from the star, reaching values “as high as 120 K in the innermost disk regions and as low as 20 K at the disk outer edge” (Isella et al. 2018). Finally, the global motion of the gas follows an ordered rotation dominated by stellar gravity: “The overall kinematics of the CO gas is well described by Keplerian rotation around a star with a mass of about 2.0 M⊙” (Isella et al. 2018). Unlike the dust, the gas does not trace prominent rings and gaps, but instead appears in a smoother way, since “The maps of the 12CO, 13CO, and C18O J = 2 − 1 emission do not show such structures but reveal a change in the slope of the radial intensity profile across the positions of the dark rings in the continuum image” (Isella et al. 2016). This shows that what matters is not only how much gas is present, but also how it is redistributed relative to the dust, because “we deduce that the gas-to-dust ratio varies across the disk and, in particular, it increases by at least a factor 5 within the inner dust gap compared to adjacent regions of the disk” (Isella et al. 2016), suggesting that in this inner region the dust is more depleted than the gas.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
The architecture of HD 163296, particularly its gaps and rings, is linked to real perturbations in the disk gas rather than to changes in dust opacity alone, as shown by the fact that “The recent detection of deviations from Keplerian rotation at the location of the gaps and rings by Teague et al. (2018) confirms that these structures correspond to variations in the gas pressure (i.e., variations in the gas density and/or temperature), as opposed to radial changes in the dust opacity” (Isella et al. 2018). In this context, comparisons between the observations and planet–disk interaction models suggest that several of these gaps may have been carved by planets, since “The comparison between the observations and planet–disk interaction models indicates that the density gaps might have been carved by planets with masses between 0.5 and 1 MJ orbiting at 48, 86, and 131 au from the central star” (Isella et al. 2018). The paper also shows that this evidence does not rely on a single scenario, because under very low-viscosity conditions “the observed multiple ring structure may also result from the gravitational interaction with a single planet that is less massive than Saturn at 100 au orbital radius” (Isella et al. 2018). In addition, substructures such as the crescent observed inside D45 reinforce this interpretation, since “features similar to the crescent observed inside the D45 gap (inset b of Figure 1) are naturally produced by the planet–disk interaction whenever the disk viscosity is low (α < 10−3) and the planet mass is above about 0.1 MJ” (Isella et al. 2018). Finally, the continuum asymmetries are not interpreted merely as morphological details, but as further support for this hypothesis, because “these asymmetries trace local variations in the dust densities and support the hypothesis that the HD 163296 disk is shaped by the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets” (Isella et al. 2018). This interpretation is extended by recent dynamical studies, in which HD 163296 “has been proposed to host at least four giant planets on wide orbits as well as a massive planetesimal disc” (Polychroni et al. 2025), so that the evidence for planet formation is not limited to the observed morphology, but also includes its consequences for the solid material in the system. Within that framework, “in our simulations the formation of giant planets in the HD 163296 system creates a large population of dynamically excited planetesimals, the majority of which originate from beyond the CO snowline” (Polychroni et al. 2025), and “HD 163296’s giant planets between 50 and 260 au are capable of delivering large amounts of planetesimals inwards of 40 au” (Polychroni et al. 2025), suggesting that the presence of young giant planets may not only sculpt rings and gaps, but also redistribute material toward the inner regions of the system.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of HD 163296
HD 163296 can be physically interpreted as a disk in which dust is not distributed uniformly, but instead tends to accumulate in specific regions of the gas. In particular, “Dullemond et al. (2018) finds that many of the continuum rings revealed by DSHARP observations, including the rings B67 and B100 of the HD 163296 disk, have a radial width that is narrower than the estimated pressure scale height of the gaseous disk, and conclude that this is strong evidence that these rings have formed by radial migration of dust particles toward local maxima of the gas pressure” (Isella et al. 2018). This suggests that the rings are not simply patterns seen in the image, but regions where dust has migrated and accumulated because the local gas pressure favors it. At the same time, this architecture develops within a thin disk, and the outer region is not perfectly uniform, since “The disc is shown to be thin … Significant modulation of the intensity of the outer ring emission is revealed and discussed” (Diep et al. 2019). This complexity cannot be explained by smooth dust trapping alone, because “the observed asymmetries in the dust continuum emission corroborate the hypothesis that the complex structure of the HD 163296 disk is the result of the gravitational interaction with yet-unseen planets” (Isella et al. 2018). Meanwhile, the gas preserves an overall ordered motion, given that “The three line emissions display remarkably similar kinematics dominated by Keplerian rotation. An upper limit of 9% of the rotation velocity has been placed on a possible in-fall velocity” (Diep et al. 2019). Taken together, these results suggest a system in which the rings arise from dust concentration at gas-pressure maxima, while the more complex structures, such as asymmetries and brightness modulations, point to active interaction between the disk and possible forming planets.

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
AS 209 is located in the Ophiuchus molecular cloud, a nearby star-forming region that hosts many young stellar systems. In the literature, the source is described as a T Tauri star in the young Ophiuchus star-forming region at a distance of 126 pc from the Sun (Fedele et al. 2018).
Its relative proximity makes AS 209 an excellent target for high-resolution millimeter observations, allowing both the dust and gas substructures of the disk to be studied in detail.

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
General description of the system
AS 209 is a young protoplanetary system surrounding a T Tauri star, with a stellar mass of 0.9 M☉, spectral type K5, and luminosity of 1.5 L☉ (Tazzari et al. 2016), located in the Ophiuchus star-forming region at a distance of 126 pc (Fedele et al. 2018). At 1.3 mm, Fedele et al. show that its disk displays a bright central core, two prominent rings at ~75 and ~130 au, and two gaps at ~62 and ~103 au, with the inner gap being narrower and shallower than the outer one (Fedele et al. 2018). Building on this observational picture, Zhang et al. highlight the “excellent agreement between observations and simulations for AS 209” and describe it as “a system with many gaps,” reinforcing the idea that it is a particularly valuable object for studying disk substructures and their possible connection to planet formation (Zhang et al. 2018). In addition, the multiwavelength analysis of Tazzari et al. reveals evidence for radial dust evolution: the largest grains are concentrated in the inner regions, whereas smaller grains dominate the outer disk, suggesting that the growth and redistribution of solids do not occur uniformly throughout the disk (Tazzari et al. 2016).

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
AS 209 exhibits a radial architecture with multiple gaps, as noted by Zhang et al.: “Huang et al. (2018a) and Guzmán et al. (2018) identified many gaps in this system including dark annuli at 9, 24, 35, 61, 90, 105, and 137 au” (Zhang et al. 2018). Not all of these substructures have the same depth, depending on their location within the system. For example, the authors indicate that “We find that the gap at r = 61 au is shallower than the main gap, and it is at 0.5–0.7 rp. Thus, we treat it as a secondary gap induced by the planet” (Zhang et al. 2018). In other words, the gap at 61 au would be less deep than the main gap around 100 au. Within that same interpretive framework, Zhang et al. add: “Our simulation with one planet at 99 au not only matches the primary gap around 100 au, but also matches the position and amplitude of secondary (61 au), tertiary (35 au), and even the fourth (24 au) inner gaps” (Zhang et al. 2018). This morphology is also clearly reflected in the dust continuum observations presented by Fedele et al.: “The dust continuum emission is characterized by a main central core and two prominent rings at r = 75 au and r = 130 au intervaled by two gaps at r = 62 au and r = 103 au” (Fedele et al. 2018). The rings of AS 209 also show: “The continuum emission is characterized by a bright central emission and two weaker dust rings peaking at ∼75 and 130 au” (Fedele et al. 2018); thus, the continuum emission maxima are located in those two rings. The annular substructures in the system are not continuous, but are instead separated by narrow gaps with lower emission. In general, the architecture of AS 209 is described as follows: “We find that the 1.3 mm continuum brightness distribution of AS 209 can be explained very well by a profile with two deep gaps … at 62 and 103 au, respectively, and an excess ring … at ∼130 au” (Fedele et al. 2018).

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
The observation and study of dust dynamics in protoplanetary disks are grounded in the fact that “High angular resolution observations at several (sub-)mm wavelengths constitute the ideal tool with which to directly probe the bulk of dust grains and to investigate the radial distribution of their properties” (Tazzari et al. 2016). In this context, it is well established that dust is not distributed uniformly, since “Theoretical models of grain growth predict that dust properties change as a function of disk radius, mass, age, and other physical conditions” (Tazzari et al. 2016). In the case of AS 209, this radial variation is closely linked to grain size: “In these disks we find evidence of a decrease in the maximum dust grain size, amax, with radius. We derive large amax values up to 1 cm in the inner disk 15 AU ≤ R ≤ 30 AU and smaller grains with amax ∼ 1 mm in the outer disk (R ≳ 80 AU)” (Tazzari et al. 2016). Fedele et al. further show that this distribution changes not only with radius, but also from one disk substructure to another, since “the inner gap is partially filled with millimeter grains while the outer gap is largely devoid of dust” (Fedele et al. 2018), indicating that dust depletion is not equally severe in all gaps. The reason for this behavior is that “radial drift effectively depletes the large grain population in disks within 1 Myr … unless it is halted by the occurrence of dust traps” (Tazzari et al. 2016); indeed, Fedele et al. explain that “the millimeter dust grains are radially confined regions in which inward radial migration is slowed down or stopped” and that “dust traps provide the ideal environment … allowing dust to grow in situ” (Fedele et al. 2018). In AS 209, these dust traps help explain both the retention of large grains in the disk and their accumulation in rings, within a scenario in which “the planet is responsible for the gap opening and for the pile-up of dust at the outer edge of the planet orbit” (Fedele et al. 2018).

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
In AS 209, the gas is also not distributed uniformly, and its structure becomes clearer when different molecular tracers are examined together. The C\(^{18}\)O emission first reveals a well-defined radial organization, with an inner concentration of gas close to the star and an outer ring around 140 au, separated by a region of weaker emission: “The C18O emission shows a compact (≲60 au), centrally peaked emission and an outer ring peaking at 140 au” (Favre et al. 2019). In addition, its radial profile “harbors a drop in intensity between the two continuum gaps” (Favre et al. 2019), suggesting that the substructures observed in the dust are also reflected in the gas. This connection is further strengthened by Fedele et al., who note that “the ALMA C18O image presented in Huang et al. (2016) shows an extended emission peaking at nearly 130 au. This extended emission is co-spatial to the outer dust ring” (Fedele et al. 2018), indicating that the outer dust ring also has a gaseous counterpart. Along the same line, the authors add that “This is a strong indication that the large scale C18O emission follows the actual disk surface density in the outer disk and it points to a gas density drop by a factor of a few inside the outer gap (‘G2’)” (Fedele et al. 2018), reinforcing the idea that the outer gap is not only a dust substructure, but also a region where the gas density decreases. In contrast, DCO\(^+\) does not follow exactly the same pattern: “Regarding the DCO+ emission, two rings are observed” (Favre et al. 2019), one between the two dust gaps and another beyond the outer gap, showing that different tracers reveal different physical and chemical conditions within the disk. To explain this overall morphology, Favre et al. conclude that “Σgas needs to be lowered” inside the gaps (Favre et al. 2019), meaning that temperature or opacity effects alone are not sufficient and that a real decrease in the gas surface density is required. Taken together, this evidence supports a dynamical interpretation of the disk, in which the gaseous structure of AS 209 has been modified by a “gas perturbation due to one or more forming planets” (Favre et al. 2019).

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
In AS 209, the evidence for planet formation does not rest on a single isolated substructure, but rather on the consistent way in which observations and models describe the disk as a dynamically perturbed system. In the millimeter continuum, it had already been proposed that “the observed rings and gaps of the 1.3 mm continuum emission are likely due to the formation of planets; this hypothesis is commensurate with hydrodynamical simulations” (Favre et al. 2019), meaning that the ringed architecture of the disk is consistent with the action of forming planets. This interpretation is further strengthened by the results of Zhang et al.: “our simulation with one planet at 99 au not only matches the primary gap around 100 au, but also matches the position and amplitude of secondary (61 au), tertiary (35 au), and even the fourth (24 au) inner gaps” (Zhang et al. 2018). In other words, a single embedded planet could account not only for the main gap, but also for several inner substructures in the disk. For this reason, the authors conclude that “This makes AS 209 the most plausible case that there is indeed a planet within the 100 au gap” (Zhang et al. 2018). Moreover, the evidence does not come from the dust alone: Favre et al. add that “Our findings confirm that the previously observed dust gaps are very likely due to perturbation of the gas surface density that is induced by a planet of at least 0.2MJ in formation” (Favre et al. 2019), which reinforces the interpretation that the observed gaps trace a real planet–disk interaction process rather than a merely apparent redistribution of material.

title: AS 209: Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of AS 209
AS 209 can be understood as a young disk in which dust evolution and planet–disk interaction are operating together. On the one hand, the dust does not evolve uniformly across the system: larger grains are concentrated toward the inner regions, while smaller grains dominate farther out in the disk, a pattern that is consistent with grain growth, radial drift, and the need for retention mechanisms to keep solids from being rapidly lost. On the other hand, the observed ring-and-gap architecture is best explained if the disk is being dynamically sculpted by an embedded planet in a very low-viscosity environment, since Zhang et al. show that “the q = 10−4 planet in a α = 10−5 and h/r = 0.05 disk can explain all five gaps at 24, 35, 62, 90, and 105 au” (Zhang et al. 2018), implying that a single outer planet could account for much of the system’s radial structure. This interpretation becomes even stronger because the gas is also genuinely perturbed: Favre et al. conclude that “the previously observed dust gaps are very likely due to perturbation of the gas surface density that is induced by a planet of at least 0.2MJ in formation” (Favre et al. 2019), showing that the gaps are not merely brightness variations in the dust, but signatures of a real reorganization of the disk surface density. Taken together, AS 209 is best interpreted as a low-turbulence disk in which a forming planet is already reshaping both the dust and the gas, leaving an observable imprint of ongoing planet formation.

title: Activities
text:
This section presents introductory programming activities based on real protoplanetary disk data. The aim is to help students connect scientific interpretation with practical work using astronomical images.
Esta sección presenta actividades introductorias de programación basadas en datos reales de discos protoplanetarios. El objetivo es ayudar a los estudiantes a conectar la interpretación científica con el trabajo práctico usando imágenes astronómicas.
The activities focus on FITS images, disk geometry, and simple analysis tools that make it possible to identify rings, gaps, and other disk substructures.
Las actividades se centran en imágenes FITS, geometría de discos y herramientas simples de análisis que permiten identificar anillos, brechas y otras subestructuras del disco.

title: Activities
text:
Activity 1 / Actividad 1

English
Radial profile from a FITS image
In this activity, students work with a FITS image of a protoplanetary disk and compute a radial brightness profile. The goal is to identify how the intensity changes with distance from the central star and relate those changes to rings, gaps, or other disk substructures.

Suggested materials - FITS image of a disk - Notebook or Python script - Activity rubric - Short guide with the required libraries
What students practice - Reading FITS files - Visualizing astronomical images - Measuring intensity as a function of radius - Interpreting rings and gaps from data
Files - Dataset folder - Rubric - Notebook or script



Español
Perfil radial a partir de una imagen FITS
En esta actividad, los estudiantes trabajan con una imagen FITS de un disco protoplanetario y calculan un perfil radial de brillo. El objetivo es identificar cómo cambia la intensidad con la distancia a la estrella central y relacionar esas variaciones con anillos, brechas u otras subestructuras del disco.

Materiales sugeridos - Imagen FITS de un disco - Notebook o script en Python - Rúbrica de la actividad - Guía breve con las librerías necesarias
Qué se practica - Lectura de archivos FITS - Visualización de imágenes astronómicas - Medición de intensidad en función del radio - Interpretación de anillos y brechas a partir de los datos
Archivos - Carpeta de datos - Rúbrica - Notebook o script

title: Activities
text:
Activity 2 / Actividad 2

English
Deprojection and polar analysis of a disk image
In this activity, students use a disk image to apply a geometric deprojection and then transform it into polar coordinates. This makes it easier to identify rings, asymmetries, and localized structures in the disk.

Suggested materials - FITS image of an inclined disk - Notebook or Python script - Activity rubric - Short methodological guide
What students practice - Using inclination and position angle - Deprojecting a disk image - Converting an image into polar coordinates - Identifying substructures more clearly
Files - Dataset folder - Rubric - Notebook or script



Español
Deproyección y análisis polar de una imagen de disco
En esta actividad, los estudiantes usan una imagen de un disco para aplicar una deproyección geométrica y luego transformarla a coordenadas polares. Esto facilita la identificación de anillos, asimetrías y estructuras localizadas en el disco.

Materiales sugeridos - Imagen FITS de un disco inclinado - Notebook o script en Python - Rúbrica de la actividad - Guía metodológica breve
Qué se practica - Uso de inclinación y ángulo de posición - Deproyección de una imagen de disco - Conversión de una imagen a coordenadas polares - Identificación más clara de subestructuras

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
AS 209 is located in the Ophiuchus star-forming region, at a distance of 126 pc from the Sun (Fedele et al. 2018). Ophiuchus is a particularly valuable environment for the study of protoplanetary disks, as it hosts a rich population of young stellar systems observed across different evolutionary stages. Its significant number of deeply embedded sources, many with ages below about 1 Myr, makes this region especially well suited for investigating how circumstellar disks emerge and evolve during the earliest phases of star and planet formation.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
General description of the system
HL Tau is a remarkably important young system because, although it is still described as a “Class I–II protostar” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), it already hosts a compact, massive disk together with a highly collimated outflow, reflecting an early yet highly active stage of evolution. ALMA observations revealed a clear sequence of “bright and dark rings” in the disk (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), showing that a complex architecture is already in place at this initial stage. Moreover, the dark regions are “not devoid of emission” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), and their properties are consistent with dust growth and evolution, suggesting that they may be linked to the earliest stages of planet formation. This interpretation becomes clearer when one considers that ALMA primarily traces millimeter-sized dust: in the presence of embedded protoplanets, that dust can respond differently from the gas, making the gaps appear sharper in the dust component (“different response of the dust compared to the gas”; “it is easier to carve gaps in dust than in gas” (Dipierro et al. 2015)). Along the same lines, radiative transfer modeling indicates that the main gaps are “depleted by at least a factor of 10” in dust and that the outermost ring contains fewer millimeter-sized grains than the inner regions, pointing to more efficient grain growth closer to the star and/or radial migration (Pinte et al. 2016). In addition, the millimeter dust forms a “geometrically thin (scale height ≈1 AU at 100 AU)” layer (Pinte et al. 2016), which is interpreted as a clear sign of strong settling toward the midplane, precisely where solid material can become concentrated and continue to evolve.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
The morphology of the HL Tau disk is defined by a remarkably pronounced annular architecture, resolved by ALMA in unprecedented detail at 2.9, 1.3, and 0.87 mm. These observations reveal a pattern of “bright and dark rings observed at all wavelengths” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), demonstrating that these are genuine disk substructures rather than imaging artifacts. In the combined 1.0 mm image, the authors identify “seven pairs of ‘bright’ and ‘dark’ rings” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), organized as B1–B7 for the bright rings and D1–D7 for the dark gaps, revealing a complex and highly ordered radial distribution of material. Dipierro et al. (2015) further describe HL Tau as showing “concentric, axisymmetric gaps,” emphasizing the regular and symmetric character of this morphology. In their interpretation, this appearance can be understood because ALMA primarily traces millimeter-sized dust, and that component does not respond to embedded planets in the same way as the gas; as a result, while the gas may develop spiral structure, the dust tends to highlight rings and gaps because of the “different response of gas and dust” (Dipierro et al. 2015). In addition, the planets in their model are not presented as direct detections, but rather as a dynamical test of plausibility: the main observed morphology can be reproduced with “three embedded planets at 13.2, 32.3 and 68.8 au” (Dipierro et al. 2015), supporting the idea that the most prominent gaps may be linked to planet–disk interaction.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
In HL Tau, dust is not distributed homogeneously, but instead exhibits a clearly differentiated radial and vertical structure. ALMA observations indicate a “pattern of bright and dark rings observed at all wavelengths,” and that the dark rings “are not devoid of emission” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), which indicates that the solid material does not disappear completely in those regions, but is instead redistributed within the disk. On the one hand, Pinte et al. show that the spectral index increases from the inner to the outer regions, indicating that the emission properties change with radius. In this context, the fact that the outermost ring is depleted in millimeter-sized dust is interpreted as evidence of “faster grain growth in the central regions and/or radial migration of the larger grains” (Pinte et al. 2016). Added to this is the fact that the main gaps show a drastic reduction in dust density relative to the adjacent rings, indicating that the solid material does not form a smooth disk, but is already strongly concentrated in some regions and depleted in others. Moreover, the millimeter dust is confined to a “geometrically thin (scale height ≈1 AU at 100 AU)” (Pinte et al. 2016) layer, which constitutes a clear sign of vertical settling toward the midplane. Taken together, this suggests that the millimeter continuum is dominated by large, settled grains, whereas reproducing the SED requires an additional population of submicron grains distributed in a much thicker structure, thus reinforcing the idea of strong dust stratification in the disk.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
In HL Tau, the disk gas is not observed as a simple, isolated component, but rather as part of a system that remains embedded in an active environment. ALMA observations show that its study is affected by “spatial and kinematic confusion due to the extended ridge and outflow emission near the ambient velocity of vlsr = 6–7 km s−1” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), meaning that the disk signal is partially blended with that of the surrounding material. Even so, HCO+ made it possible to “spatially resolve the morphology of the HL Tau molecular gas disk for the first time” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), revealing a “roughly Keplerian” velocity distribution over a detectable range of 2.0 to 12.0 km s−1. Along the same lines, Pinte et al. show that both HCO+ and ^12CO display “a spatially compact component” and a “velocity gradient consistent with disk rotation” (Pinte et al. 2016), confirming that the brightest gas is truly associated with the disk and rotates around the star. Moreover, not all tracers describe this dynamics in the same way: CN and HCN “are only detected in absorption” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), and part of the observed asymmetry is interpreted as self-absorption linked to the outflow. Taken together, this picture shows that the gas in HL Tau follows an approximately Keplerian rotation, although its structure and kinematics are not entirely simple; indeed, while ALMA Partnership derives a stellar mass of about ~1.3 M☉, Pinte et al. conclude that the gas kinematics are more consistent with a star of ~1.7 M☉, reinforcing the view that this is a young, dynamic molecular disk whose detailed interpretation still requires further modeling.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
Observationally, HL Tau provides evidence of planet formation: thanks to ALMA, we know that the disk material is already being dynamically sculpted. The disk gaps “are not devoid of emission” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), which means that the cavity is not empty, but instead corresponds to a region where the dust has been redistributed by a physical process consistent with planet–disk interaction. In addition, several rings show center offsets, and some substructures follow resonant proportions such as D1:D2:D3:D4 = 1:4:6:8; these dynamics—opening gaps, perturbing the orbits of disk material, and organizing structures within the disk—are evidence of a protoplanet altering the disk architecture. This interpretation is further supported by the simulations of Dipierro et al. (2015), in which “three embedded planets” were modeled at 13.2, 32.3, and 68.8 au, with masses of 0.2, 0.27, and 0.55 MJ, and it was concluded that this scenario can “reproduce most of the structures seen in the ALMA image”. In other words, the planets are not presented here as directly detected objects, but as a physically plausible explanation for why the disk displays such an ordered architecture: the protoplanets open gaps, push dust toward the edges of those gaps, and—because the dust responds more clearly than the gas—the final image observed by ALMA naturally takes on this morphology of bright and dark rings.

title: HL Tau:Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation
HL Tau can be physically interpreted as an extremely young system that remains in an active evolutionary stage, yet whose disk already exhibits a complex architecture that is unlikely to arise from a passive distribution of material alone. As ALMA observations describe it, HL Tau is “an excellent example of a system just emerging from its protostellar cocoon” (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015), and even at this stage it already shows bright rings, dark gaps, evidence of grain growth, and a radial organization suggesting that both dust and gas are being dynamically rearranged. In this context, Dipierro et al. provide a broader physical interpretation: the morphology of HL Tau can be understood as the outcome of interactions between dust, gas, and embedded planets, with the millimeter dust responding more visibly than the gas to gravitational perturbations and therefore producing the sharply defined rings and gaps seen by ALMA. Taken together, these studies converge on the same picture: HL Tau is not simply a young disk with substructures, but a system in which dust evolution, material redistribution, and the possible presence of protoplanets may already be actively sculpting the disk architecture.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
What is a protoplanetary disk?
A protoplanetary disk is a young, flattened structure that forms around a star during its earliest stages, when part of the collapsing material no longer falls directly onto the protostar but instead settles into orbit through “angular momentum conservation” (Williams and Cieza 2011; Andrews 2020). In this sense, it can be understood as a “flattened disk of gas and solids” that accompanies the young star and gathers the material from which the system will continue to evolve. These disks are also the “material reservoirs and birthplaces of planetary systems,” because they host the processes that may eventually lead to the formation of planets (Andrews 2020).
Their connection to young stars is therefore direct: they appear “shortly after their birth” and are studied especially around visible young stars such as T Tauri stars, during early phases like Class II YSOs (Williams and Cieza 2011). In general, they “form almost immediately after a molecular core collapses,” and they show “an inherent and large diversity in initial disk sizes and masses” (Williams and Cieza 2011). Once the initial envelope has dispersed, the system is already “truly protoplanetary, not protostellar”; at that stage, the disk contains only “a few percent of the central stellar mass,” usually has a “flared” geometry, and shows approximately “Keplerian” motions, features that describe its basic shape and dynamics (Williams and Cieza 2011).
These disks not only store material, but also provide the setting where planet formation begins. In the “core accretion model,” dust grains grow through several stages until they become rocks, planetesimals, and protoplanetary cores capable of capturing gas (Williams and Cieza 2011). For that reason, studying the mass, structure, and evolution of a disk helps us understand not only how a young system changes over time, but also what kinds of planets may form within it and how their properties depend on the disk’s physical conditions (Andrews 2020).

How do we observe protoplanetary disks?
Protoplanetary disks are studied at different wavelengths because each tracer reveals a different part of their structure (Andrews 2020). In the optical and near-infrared, “scattered light” primarily traces small dust grains in the disk surface; in the mid-infrared, warmer dust in the inner regions is more easily detected; and at sub-mm/mm wavelengths, “thermal continuum emission” is used to study the distribution of colder solids, while “spectral line emission” probes the properties and dynamics of the gas (Andrews 2020). Because many disks are cold, small on the sky, and emit efficiently at millimeter wavelengths, “radio interferometry” has become essential, and much of the recent progress in the field has been driven by ALMA, one of the most important tools for studying protoplanetary disks and their substructures (Andrews 2020).

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Physical processes shaping protoplanetary disks
The structure and evolution of protoplanetary disks are governed by several fundamental physical processes.

Disk accretion and angular momentum transport
Gas in a disk orbits the central star approximately in Keplerian rotation. However, for material to accrete onto the star, it must lose angular momentum. This redistribution of angular momentum drives disk evolution.
As Andrews notes, disk structure is deeply connected with disk dynamics and evolution (Andrews 2020). Theoretical models commonly describe these systems within the framework of thin accretion disks (Armitage 2011).


Gravitational instability
In sufficiently massive and cold disks, self-gravity can become important. In this regime, gravitational instabilities can generate spiral density patterns that redistribute mass and angular momentum within the disk.
These spiral structures may also concentrate solid particles and potentially contribute to the formation of planetesimals.


Pressure traps and dust concentration
In many disks, local gas-pressure maxima can trap drifting solids. These regions are especially important because they help explain why dust can accumulate in rings instead of being lost rapidly through inward radial drift (Andrews 2020).

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Substructures in protoplanetary disks
High-resolution observations have revealed that protoplanetary disks frequently contain substructures, meaning localized variations in the distribution of gas or dust.
These structures include rings, gaps, spirals, and asymmetric features. Their discovery has significantly reshaped our understanding of disk evolution and planet formation (Andrews 2020).
Substructures are particularly important because they can create local pressure maxima that trap drifting particles. In smooth disks, solid particles tend to migrate inward rapidly due to aerodynamic drag from the gas. However, pressure maxima can halt this inward drift and concentrate particles, potentially enabling the formation of planetesimals.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Common substructure morphologies
Observations have identified several common classes of disk substructures.

Rings and gaps
This is the most common morphology observed in millimeter continuum images. It consists of concentric bright rings separated by darker gaps.


Cavities
Some systems display a large central cavity surrounded by an outer ring of emission. This morphology is commonly associated with transition disks.


Arcs and crescents
Some disks show asymmetric arc-shaped or crescent-like structures, which may correspond to vortices or local dust concentrations.


Spirals
Large-scale spiral patterns can arise from gravitational instability or perturbations produced by massive companions or forming planets.

Figure 6: Composite image of the planet-forming disk MWC 758, combining infrared observations from the SPHERE instrument on ESO’s Very Large Telescope with millimeter observations from ALMA.
